content.full_text:
Re
Farn PoLrrrcAL PnacrrcEs CouMrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
September 10,2003
Colleen C. McAndrews
Bell, McAndrews, Hiltachk & Davidian,LLP
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, CA 95814
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-03-197
Dear Ms. McAndrews:
You have requested advice on behalf of Californians for Schwarzenegger and
Arnold Schwarzenegger's Total Recall Committee, Vote Yes to Recall Gray Davis,
regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note
that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have
already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975)
I FPPC ops. 71.)
QUESTIONS
1. Do electronic contributor records collected by Calnet Business Bank on behalf
of califomians for schwarzenegger and Arnold schwarzenegger's Total
Recall committee, vote Yes to Recall Gray Davis (the "committees") meet
the recordkeeping requirements of the Act?
2. Will the committees comply with the recordkeeping requirements of the Act if
bank and contributor records are provided to and maintained by the
committees on CD-ROM?
CONCLUSIONS
1. and 2. Yes. The electronic records described in your letter and discussed
below appear to meet the Act's recordkeeping requirements.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109 - 18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


Our File No. A-03-197
Page 2
FACTS
The committees have opened campaign bank accounts with Calnet Business Bank
(the "bank"). The bank has been in existence for approximately one and one-half years.
Its business model places an emphasis on being technologically advanced. To that end,
the bank uses processes for collecting contributions that utilize electronic means for data
collection and storage. The bank is directly receiving contributions on behalf of the
committees and will provide source documentation to the committees using the following
methods:
1. Contributions made by credit card and electronic checl<s via the Internet
Contributions to the committees are being made over the Intemet using either
credit cards or electronic checks. The contributor must input name, address, occupation,
employer, telephone number, email address, and the amount of the contribution before
the contribution can be accepted and processed.
The bank validates all of the credit card
information against the data entered by the contributor and if it matches, the bank
approves the contribution.
If the contribution is accepted, the transaction is marked with the date of receipt
and assigned a unique transaction number traceable back to the contributor's bank. The
contribution information then appears on the bank's secure website, viewable only by
select committee staff. The data will be transferred to a CD-ROM and maintained by the
committees.
2. Contributions made by credit card viafox
Contributors may make contributions by credit card by completing a donor form
and faxing it directly to the bank. The bank receives the fax using a line dedicated solely
to the committees and enters the transaction on the date of receipt. The contribution
information is processed and posted to the secure website in the same manner as
contributions made via the Internet. No contributions will be accepted with missing
donor information. The bank will send the committees copies of the faxes either on paper
or on a CD-ROM.
3. Contrtbutions made by check via mail and Federal Express
The bank maintains a secure post office box for the committees. Contributors
may mail contributions directly to this box or may send contributions directly to the bank
via Federal Express. The bank receives the checks, and will acceptthem if the donors
have properly completed a response device that asks for the required donor information.


Our File No. A-03-197
Page 3
All of the data collected by the bank that is required to be disclosed on the
committees' campaign reports will be uploaded to the committees'reporting software in
order to produce the campaign reports.
ANALYSIS
Section 84104 contains the Act's recordkeeping requirements:
"It shall be the duty of each candidate, treasurer and
elected officer to maintain such detailed accounts, records,
bills and receipts that are necessary to prepare carnpaign
statements and to comply with the provisions of this
chapter. The detailed accounts, records, bills and receipts
that are maintained shall be retained by the filer for a
period specified by the Commission ... ."
Regulation 18401 (copy enclosed) sets forth the records that committees must
maintain for contributions.
Contributions under $25. Candidates and treasurers must keep a list of the dates
and daily totals of contributions received under $25. The original source documentation
for these contributions consists of bank statements, check registers, bank or passbooks,
and any other records reflecting a continuous computation of campaign account balances.
(Regulation I 8a01 (a)(1).)
Contributions of $25 or more but less than $100. In addition to the daily totals
required for contributions under $25, for contributions of $25 or more but less than $100,
candidates and treasurers must keep records of the date of each contribution, the amount,
and the full name and street address of the contributor. The records must also contain the
cumulative amount received from the contributor and specify whether the contribution is
monetary or nonmon etary. (Regulation 1 8a0 1 (a)(2).)
The original source documentation for these contributions includes bank
statements, check registers and passbooks, discussed above, and copies of contributor's
checks, cashier's checks, money orders, wire transfers, deposits or duplicate deposit slips,
and any other documents reflecting all items deposited and all deposits made to any
campaign bank account. Source documentation includes contributor cards, letters of
transmittal and notices received from contributors. (Regulation 18401(a)(z)@).)
2 During our telephone conversation on Septemb er 2,2003,you confirmed that the bank will
provide copies of both the response devices and contributor shecks on paper or on CD-ROM.
The bank will provide the committees copies of the checks and response devices on paper
or on a CD-ROM containing images of the checks.2


Our File No. A-03-197
Page 4
Contributions of $100 or more. For these contributions, candidates and
treasurers must maintain all of the information required for contributions above $25, but
less than $100, and in addition, must include the occupation and employer of any
contributor (or if the contributor is self-employed, the name of the principal place of
business of the contributor). (Regulation 18401(a)(3XA).) Section 85700, added to the
Act in 2001by Proposition 34,requires that a contribution of $100 or more must be
retumed if the candidate or committee does not have in its records, within 60 days of
receipt of the contribution, the name, address, and, if applicable, the occupation and
employer of the contributor.
The original source documentation for these contributions consists of all items
listed above for smaller contributions, and in addition, all communications sent by the
committee to obtain the occupation and employer information. (Regulation
18401(a)(3XB).)
Retention of Records. Records and documents required under the Act must be
retained by candidates and treasurers for a period of four years following the date the
related campaign statement is filed or, in the case of an elected state officer serving a
four-year term, records related to campaign statements filed during the first year
following the elected officer's election must be retained for five years from the date the
campaign statement is filed. (Regulation 18a01@)(2).)
Nothing in the Act or regulation 18401 expressly requires that committee records
be kept on paper. 'Ihe 1995/96 Campaign Information Manual for candidates and their
controlled committees (Manual A) discusses regulation 18401's recordkeeping
requirements in detail and provides a sample recordkeeping system. The manual states
that the system can be adapted to a computer or other recordkeeping system. (Manual A,
page79.)
We have previously advised that contributions may be made by credit card, by
means of a tele-network using the telephone company as the collection agent, through the
use of a 90O-number telephone line, and over the Internet, provided that the Act's
disclosure and recordkeeping requirements are met. (Buck-Walsh Advice Letter, No. I-
90-239; Rittenhouse Advice Letter, No. A-93-189; Zerbe Advice Letter, No. I-95-146;
Lavin Advice Letter, No. 4-99-201; Bergeron Advice Letter, No. I-00-089; Nguyen-Tan
Advice Letter, No. A-00-168.) The letters cited point out the following important
information committee treasurers should keep in mind for recordkeeping and disclosure
purposes:
Section 8a300(c) requires contributions to be made by "written instrument,"
containing the name of the donor and the name of the recipient.
Section 84306 requires that all contributions received by a person acting as an
agent of a candidate or committee be reported promptly to the candidate or
committee treasurer. "Promptly" means no later than the closing date of any
campaign statement the candidate or committee is required to file.
a
a


Our File No. 4-03-197
Page 5
The entire amount authoized by the contributor is the amount of the contribution;
the recipient would not subtract any fees retained by the credit card company,
telephone company, or other provider.
The date a contribution is received is the date the transaction is authorized by the
contributor, not the date the committee actually receives the monetary
contribution.
When contributions are collected and transmitted to a committee by an
intermediary, the committee also must keep records of and disclose information
about the intermediary. (See section84302; regulations L8a0L@)(2XA) and
t8432.s.)
The system devised by the bank and the committees appears to meet the
requirements of section84704 and regulation 18401 that contributions received be
accurately and completely recorded and disclosed in a timely manner. Although
regulation 18401 requires that original source documents include "copies" of contributor
checks, we see no reason why images of the checks received directly by the bank cannot
be provided to the committees and maintained on a CD-ROM rather than on paper, as
long as the bank can provide actual or verified copies of the checks if necessary. As with
any recordkeeping system, receipts and expenditures should be recorded in a timely and
uniform fashion that ensures the accuracy and reliability of information. (Manual A,
page79.) Finally, the committees are responsible for ensuring that the CD-ROM records
can be read and/or printed for auditing purposes during the applicable retention period.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please call me at (916) 322-
s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
a
a
a
C^l- LU,^-/!,,J
By:
Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
Enclosure
